name: Strawberry Peak
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding near Socorro featuring colorful agate and jasper specimens. Free collecting with no permit required on easily accessible terrain.
description:
Strawberry Peak is a popular rockhounding destination on BLM land in Socorro County, New Mexico, offering excellent opportunities to collect agate, jasper, and quartz specimens. The site sits within the Rio Grande Rift zone where ancient volcanic activity and mineralized hydrothermal fluids created abundant silica-rich formations throughout the region. Collectors typically find quality specimens within short hiking distances from parking areas, making this an accessible spot for both beginners and experienced rockhounds.

The terrain around Strawberry Peak features desert scrubland with scattered outcrops and wash systems that have naturally exposed colorful agate nodules and banded jasper pieces. The area receives relatively little foot traffic compared to fee dig sites, giving you the chance to explore largely undisturbed ground. Specimens range from small pebbles suitable for tumbling to larger pieces suitable for display or cutting.

Geologically, this location sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic rocks and silica-rich mineral deposits are common. The combination of rhyolite host rock and secondary mineralization has created ideal conditions for agate and jasper formation. Water erosion through washes continues to expose fresh material regularly.

Best collecting is done by walking drainages and wash systems where water has concentrated specimens. Look carefully along banks and in loose gravel where material becomes easier to spot. The open desert landscape provides good visibility, though careful searching around outcrop bases often yields the best finds.
history: This area sits within the Mogollon-Datil volcanic field where Miocene-age rhyolitic volcanism (approximately 25-30 million years ago) deposited silica-rich fluids that formed the agate and jasper deposits found here today. The Rio Grande Rift extension that began in the Oligocene created the structural setting for this mineralization.
tips: Walk dry washes and drainage bottoms where water erosion naturally concentrates heavy agate and jasper specimens. Look for banded or colorful pieces contrasting with the tan-brown desert soil, particularly in loose gravel areas near outcrop bases. Use a small brush or air bulb to clean specimens in the field to verify color and banding patterns. Pack out all material you collect and fill any holes you dig to minimize surface disturbance.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 5,200-5,600 feet with desert scrubland vegetation including creosote, yucca, and scattered juniper. Terrain is gently rolling with intermittent washes and some rocky outcrops. Walking distance from parking varies from 0.5 to 2 miles depending on specific collecting areas. Footing is generally stable but uneven with scattered rocks and small boulders requiring careful stepping.
best_season: October - April. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit with intense sun exposure and minimal shade. Winter and spring offer comfortable daytime temperatures, lower UV exposure, and better conditions for extended field work.
written_directions: From Socorro, take US 60 east approximately 8 miles. Look for dirt pullouts and access points along the north side of the highway in the vicinity of Strawberry Peak. Park in established turnouts and walk north into the open BLM land. GPS coordinates (34.105587, -107.004298) mark the peak itself, but collecting occurs in the surrounding desert to the east and northeast of the main summit.
road_conditions: US 60 is paved and well-maintained. Pullouts are unpaved dirt/gravel with no significant washboard. High-clearance vehicle not required for parking area access.
parking_notes: Multiple informal dirt pullouts along US 60 with capacity for 2-4 vehicles each. No designated parking lot or facilities. Parking directly in turnouts off highway right-of-way.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No surface disturbance beyond hand collection. Leave site as you found it. Fill any test holes.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lb maximum per day
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
No heavy excavation
Fill all holes after collecting
Leave site undisturbed
nearest_services: Socorro, 8 miles west. Gas, food, water, and supplies available. Last chance for services heading east on US 60.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. Uneven rocky ground requires sturdy footwear but presents no major obstacles. Informal parking directly from highway reduces access distance. No wheelchair accessibility due to uneven desert terrain and lack of developed paths.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Strawberry Peak in New Mexico?
faq.answer: Strawberry Peak is known for colorful agate, banded jasper, quartz specimens, and chalcedony. Most collectors find small to medium agate nodules in various colors including red, brown, white, and banded varieties. Quality is generally good for tumbling material and small display pieces.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Strawberry Peak near Socorro?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Strawberry Peak sits on public BLM land where recreational collecting is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You simply need to follow the 25 lb per day collection limit and personal use only rules.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Strawberry Peak?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures in this area regularly exceed 95 degrees with intense sun and minimal shade. Spring and fall offer comfortable daytime temperatures (70-80 degrees) and lower UV exposure for extended collecting sessions.

faq.question: How far is Strawberry Peak from Socorro and what are the directions?
faq.answer: Strawberry Peak is approximately 8 miles east of Socorro on US 60. From downtown Socorro, take US 60 east and watch for dirt pullouts along the north side of the highway. The location has multiple informal parking areas in turnouts directly accessible from the highway.

faq.question: Is Strawberry Peak beginner-friendly for rockhounding in New Mexico?
faq.answer: Yes, Strawberry Peak is excellent for beginners. The terrain is relatively flat and accessible with no technical climbing required. Material is easy to spot in the open desert, and the BLM land allows free collecting without permits, making it an ideal introduction to New Mexico rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and gear should I bring to rockhound at Strawberry Peak?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick or rock hammer, a collection bag, sun protection (hat and sunscreen), and at least 2 liters of water. A small brush or air bulb helps clean specimens in the field. Sturdy hiking boots are essential on the uneven rocky terrain. In summer, early morning collecting is recommended due to intense afternoon heat.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Strawberry Peak near Socorro. Collect agate, jasper, and quartz. No permit required. Easy terrain for beginners.
